s101_to_s109.title: Cooperation with Commissioner Inspections and Investigations (Part VIII)

legal.summary: Part VIII of the PDPA (sections 101–109) confers on the Commissioner broad powers of inspection, complaint handling, and investigation. Data users are subject to corresponding duties to cooperate with the Commissioner's exercise of these powers, including duties to produce documents, facilitate access to premises, and respond to questions from authorised officers. Failure to cooperate is an offence under s131 (obstruction) and s109 (failure to cooperate with investigation), both carrying significant penalties.

requirements.requirement: Cooperate fully with any inspection or audit conducted by the Commissioner or an authorised officer under Part VIII, including facilitating access to premises, systems, and records as required
requirements.rationale: Section 101 empowers the Commissioner to conduct inspections; s131 makes obstruction an offence; cooperation is not optional
requirements.frequency: On Commissioner inspection; typically with reasonable advance notice except in urgent circumstances

requirements.requirement: Upon receipt of a complaint referral from the Commissioner, acknowledge receipt and cooperate with the Commissioner's complaint investigation, providing all requested information and documentation within the timeframes specified
requirements.rationale: Section 103 requires the Commissioner to investigate complaints; the data controller must cooperate with this process; failure to respond to complaint referrals is likely to result in an adverse finding

requirements.requirement: Produce all documents, records, and data requested by the Commissioner or an authorised officer in the course of an investigation under s107–s109, within the specified timeframe and in the requested format
requirements.rationale: Section 107 empowers the Commissioner to require production of documents; failure to produce is an offence under s109; legal professional privilege may be claimed for legitimately privileged documents

requirements.requirement: Where persons are required to attend for examination under the Commissioner's investigation powers, ensure the relevant persons attend, are truthful, and do not obstruct the examination process
requirements.rationale: Section 108 empowers the Commissioner to examine persons under oath or affirmation; providing false information is an offence under s134; obstruction is an offence under s131

technical.summary: Technical infrastructure must support the rapid retrieval and production of personal data processing records, audit logs, and system configurations required during Commissioner inspections and investigations.

requirements.requirement: Maintain an up-to-date data inventory and ROPA that can be produced to the Commissioner within 5 working days of a request during an inspection or investigation
requirements.rationale: The Commissioner's inspection powers under Part VIII are only effective if data controllers can produce accurate records; an incomplete or outdated inventory will impede the inspection and may be treated as non-cooperation
requirements.frequency: Maintained continuously; producible within 5 working days

requirements.requirement: Maintain audit logs of access to and processing of personal data that can be extracted and provided to the Commissioner on request as part of an investigation
requirements.rationale: Audit logs provide the Commissioner with the evidence needed to assess whether processing has complied with the Act; logs must be retained for a period sufficient to cover the Commissioner's investigative timeframes
requirements.frequency: Logs maintained continuously; retained for minimum 3 years

governance.summary: Data users must designate a point of contact for Commissioner interactions, establish an internal inspection response process, and brief senior management on Commissioner powers and the organisation's cooperation obligations.

requirements.requirement: Designate the DPO as the primary point of contact for all Commissioner communications, inspections, and investigations; ensure the DPO has authority to commit the organisation to compliance actions and to coordinate the organisation's response
requirements.rationale: A single designated contact ensures that Commissioner communications are not lost or fragmented across the organisation; the DPO's role under s12A includes Commissioner cooperation
requirements.frequency: Designated at DPO appointment; reviewed annually

requirements.requirement: Establish a documented Inspection Response Procedure covering: (a) Commissioner notification receipt and acknowledgement; (b) internal escalation to DPO, Legal, and Management; (c) document preservation and legal hold procedures; (d) staff briefing on cooperation obligations; (e) record-keeping of all Commissioner interactions
requirements.rationale: An inspection by the Commissioner is a significant regulatory event; an unprepared response risks non-cooperation findings, delays, and reputational damage
requirements.frequency: Established; reviewed annually and after each Commissioner interaction

legal.summary: Section 133 requires data controllers to comply with any enforcement notice issued by the Commissioner. An enforcement notice sets out specific actions the data controller must take or cease, within a specified timeframe, to remedy a contravention of the Act. Failure to comply with an enforcement notice is a criminal offence under s133 carrying up to RM1,000,000 fine and/or 3 years imprisonment (as amended by A1727).

requirements.requirement: Upon receipt of an enforcement notice from the Commissioner, immediately escalate to DPO, Legal Counsel, and senior management; assess the notice requirements and formulate a compliance plan within 7 days
requirements.rationale: Enforcement notices specify remediation timeframes that begin on receipt; delayed escalation risks non-compliance with the notice deadline

requirements.requirement: Comply with all requirements specified in the enforcement notice within the timeframe stated; document all remediation actions taken and maintain evidence of compliance
requirements.rationale: Full and timely compliance with enforcement notices is mandatory; partial or late compliance is also an offence and may result in further enforcement action including prosecution
requirements.owner: DPO / Legal Counsel / Relevant Business Units
requirements.frequency: Per enforcement notice; within specified timeframe

requirements.requirement: Where the data controller disputes the enforcement notice, seek legal advice immediately and consider whether to appeal; an appeal does not automatically stay the enforcement notice unless the Commissioner or court orders otherwise
requirements.rationale: Enforcement notices remain binding pending appeal unless a stay is granted; data controllers who ignore enforcement notices while pursuing an appeal risk offence liability
requirements.frequency: On enforcement notice receipt if dispute arises

governance.summary: Enforcement notice compliance must be tracked and reported at board level. The remediation plan must be agreed by senior management and its implementation monitored by the DPO.

requirements.requirement: Report receipt of any enforcement notice to the Board of Directors or equivalent governing body within 5 working days; provide regular compliance status updates until the notice requirements are fulfilled
requirements.rationale: Enforcement notices represent a significant regulatory risk requiring board-level oversight; board reporting ensures that adequate resources are allocated to compliance
requirements.frequency: On notice receipt; status updates as required

s135.title: Commissioner Enforcement Powers and Data Controller Response Obligations (s135)

legal.summary: Section 135 confers on the Commissioner powers to issue enforcement directions, require remediation of non-compliant processing, and take enforcement action against data controllers. Data users must respond to Commissioner enforcement powers by ceasing unlawful processing, implementing required safeguards, and cooperating with enforcement procedures. The Commissioner may require a data controller to cease processing, correct or destroy data, or impose conditions on processing. Failure to comply with Commissioner enforcement actions constitutes an offence.

requirements.requirement: Upon receiving a direction from the Commissioner under s135 to cease, modify, or restrict processing activities, comply with the direction promptly and within any timeframe specified
requirements.rationale: Commissioner enforcement directions under s135 are legally binding; non-compliance is an offence and may trigger escalated enforcement including criminal prosecution

requirements.requirement: Where required by the Commissioner, implement specific technical, organisational, or procedural safeguards as a condition of continuing processing activities
requirements.rationale: The Commissioner may impose conditions on processing as a proportionate enforcement measure; compliance with conditions is mandatory and must be evidenced and reported back to the Commissioner

s47_to_s60.title: Commissioner Appointment, Functions, and Powers — Reference (Part IV)

legal.summary: Part IV of the PDPA (sections 47–60) establishes the Personal Data Protection Commissioner, sets out the Commissioner's functions and powers, and provides for the appointment of Deputy Commissioners and authorised officers. This Part does not directly impose obligations on data controllers but provides the legal basis for the Commissioner's regulatory authority. Data users should be familiar with these provisions to understand the scope of the Commissioner's investigative and enforcement powers.

requirements.requirement: Familiarise senior management and the DPO with the Commissioner's functions under s48 (including registration, enforcement, promotion, and guidance), powers under s49 (including inspection, investigation, and enforcement direction), and authorised officer powers under s52
requirements.rationale: Understanding the Commissioner's authority enables data controllers to respond appropriately to regulatory engagement; organisations that misunderstand the scope of Commissioner powers may inadvertently obstruct lawful regulatory activity
requirements.frequency: Induction; updated when Commissioner powers change
